1. **`thm:rigidity_lemma`** — `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}`. The Rigidity Lemma:

   geometrically irreducible and a point `(v₀,w₀)`; let `α : V × W → Z` be a morphism with
   `α(V × {w₀})` a single point. Then `α` factors through the projection `V × W → W` (i.e.
   `α` depends only on the `W`-coordinate). PROVER-READY detail: this is the iter-158 prover
   entry, so give the full proof (Mumford Form I, §4 p.43 / Milne Thm 1.1): pick an affine
   open `U ∋ α(v₀,w₀)`; `V` complete ⟹ the projection `q : V × W → W` is closed; the set of
   `w` with `α(V × {w}) ⊆ U` is open and nonempty; on it `α(V × {w})` is a complete connected
   subvariety of an affine, hence a point; conclude `α` is constant on each `V`-fibre over a
   dense open, then everywhere by separatedness/irreducibility. Spell out each step.

2. **`prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant`** — `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}`.
   Every morphism `f : ℙ¹_{k̄} → A` to an abelian variety (smooth proper group scheme) is
   constant. Derive from `thm:rigidity_lemma` (Milne Prop 3.10's packaged corollary). Give the
   proof at the granularity Mumford/Milne use; if it genuinely needs the cube, state the cube

   confirm one from Mumford. (If you keep the cube, mark `thm:theorem_of_the_cube` clearly as a
   DEFERRED deep input with no full proof yet, and add a `% NOTE:`-free prose flag that it is

   proof SKETCH at textbook level (cite Hartshorne), and flag in the prose that its
   formalization is itself a sub-build (do not pretend it is short). `\lean{}` target present.

4. **`thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV`** — `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}`.
   THE HEADLINE the genus-0 arm consumes. A pointed morphism `f : C → A` (with `f` killing the
   marked point) from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-0 curve `C` over `k̄` to a
   smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme `A` over `k̄` equals the constant
   morphism at the identity. Proof: compose `prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1` with

\paragraph{Iter-135 body restructure.} The Lean body of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} is, per iter-135 refactor, a case-split $\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0$ delegating to \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (genus-$0$ arm, \texttt{sorry} body closing iter-151+ under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot --- see \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} body closure status, revised iter-145) and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} (positive-genus arm, \texttt{sorry} body closing M3 --- M3 is committed to Route A per the iter-144 disposition in STRATEGY.md \S~M3; sits behind M2 critical-path during the M2 wait window). The restructure converts the inline \texttt{sorry} on the witness-existence theorem itself into honest delegation to two named scaffolds; the witness existence is therefore conditionally established whenever both arms' bodies close (no Mathlib gap directly on the witness-existence theorem itself, only on each arm).
\end{proof}

\subsection{The genus-$0$ arm of the witness existence}
\label{sec:genusZeroWitness}

The genus-$0$ sub-case of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} admits an explicit witness construction at the Lean level, packaged separately from the general existence statement so that the genus-$0$ branch can be discharged \emph{independently of Route~A}. Its witness object is the trivial scheme $\Spec k$; the only remaining mathematical content is the rigidity statement \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}, whose committed proof route is the characteristic-free AV-rigidity lemma of \cref{sec:av_rigidity_route_c} (route~(c)), \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality route and \emph{not} the Picard representability engine.

\subsection{Route (c): rigidity via the theorem of the cube (committed genus-$0$ proof route)}
\label{sec:av_rigidity_route_c}

The genus-$0$ witness \emph{object} is the trivial scheme $\Spec k$; the only remaining mathematical content of the genus-$0$ arm is the rigidity \emph{statement} \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}. The project commits to proving it by the characteristic-free \emph{theorem-of-the-cube $\to$ rigidity-lemma} chain of Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, recorded in the blocks below. The headline fact the genus-$0$ arm consumes is \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}, the specialisation to a genus-$0$ curve of \cref{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant} (Milne, Proposition~3.10), applied to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (a rational, hence unirational, variety). This chain uses \emph{neither} $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre duality \emph{nor} the FGA $\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A. The same rigidity machinery is what the positive-genus Albanese universal property (Milne, Propositions~6.1/6.4) needs anyway, here used independently of the Picard representability.

\begin{theorem}
[Theorem of the cube]
  \label{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem 5.1 (Theorem of the Cube), \S I.5, p.~21 (PDF p.~27)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM 5.1 (THEOREM OF THE CUBE). Let $U$, $V$, $W$ be
  % complete geometrically irreducible varieties over $k$, and let
  % $u_0 \in U(k)$, $v_0 \in V(k)$, $w_0 \in W(k)$ be base points. Then an
  % invertible sheaf $L$ on $U \times V \times W$ is trivial if its
  % restrictions to $U \times V \times \{w_0\}$, $U \times \{v_0\} \times W$,
  % $\{u_0\} \times V \times W$ are all trivial."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We defer the proof until later in this section
  % [actually, until the next version]." (proof deferred in the source notes)
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem 5.1.}
  Let $U$, $V$, $W$ be complete geometrically irreducible varieties over $k$ with base points $u_0 \in U(k)$, $v_0 \in V(k)$, $w_0 \in W(k)$. An invertible sheaf $L$ on $U \times V \times W$ is trivial as soon as its restrictions to the three coordinate faces $U \times V \times \{w_0\}$, $U \times \{v_0\} \times W$, and $\{u_0\} \times V \times W$ are all trivial.
\end{theorem}

\begin{lemma}
[Rigidity Theorem]
  \label{lem:rigidity_theorem}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem 1.1 (Rigidity Theorem), \S I.1, p.~8 (PDF p.~14--15)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM 1.1 (RIGIDITY THEOREM). Consider a regular map
  % $\alpha\colon V \times W \to U$, and assume that $V$ is complete and that
  % $V \times W$ is geometrically irreducible. If there are points
  % $u_0 \in U(k)$, $v_0 \in V(k)$, and $w_0 \in W(k)$ such that
  % $\alpha(V \times \{w_0\}) = \{u_0\} = \alpha(\{v_0\} \times W)$
  % then $\alpha(V \times W) = \{u_0\}$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Since the hypotheses continue to hold after
  % extending scalars from $k$ to $k^{al}$, we can assume $k$ is
  % algebraically closed. Note that $V$ is connected, because otherwise
  % $V \times_k W$ wouldn't be connected, much less irreducible. We need to
  % use the following facts: (i) If $V$ is complete, then the projection map
  % $q\colon V \times_k W \to W$ is closed (this is the definition of being
  % complete AG 7.1). (ii) If $V$ is complete and connected, and
  % $\varphi\colon V \to U$ is a regular map from $V$ into an affine variety,
  % then $\varphi(V) = \{$ point$\}$ (AG 7.5). Let $U_0$ be an open affine
  % neighbourhood of $u_0$. Because of (i), $Z =_{df} q(\alpha^{-1}(U
  % \smallsetminus U_0))$ is closed in $W$. By definition, $Z$ consists of the
  % second coordinates of points of $V \times W$ not mapping into $U_0$. Thus a
  % point $w$ of $W$ lies outside $Z$ if and only $\alpha(V \times \{w\})
  % \subseteq U_0$. In particular $w_0$ lies outside $Z$, and so $W
  % \smallsetminus Z$ is nonempty. As $V \times \{w\}$ is complete and $U_0$ is
  % affine, $\alpha(V \times \{w\})$ must be a point whenever $w \in W
  % \smallsetminus Z$: in fact, $\alpha(V \times \{w\}) = \alpha(v_0,w) =
  % \{u_0\}$. Thus $\alpha$ is constant on the subset $V \times (W
  % \smallsetminus Z)$ of $V \times W$. As $V \times (W \smallsetminus Z)$ is
  % nonempty and open in $V \times W$, and $V \times W$ is irreducible,
  % $V \times (W \smallsetminus Z)$ is dense $V \times W$. As $U$ is separated,
  % $\alpha$ must agree with the constant map on the whole of $V \times W$."
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem 1.1.}
  \uses{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
  Let $\alpha \colon V \times W \to U$ be a regular map with $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically irreducible. If there are points $u_0 \in U(k)$, $v_0 \in V(k)$, $w_0 \in W(k)$ with $\alpha(V \times \{w_0\}) = \{u_0\} = \alpha(\{v_0\} \times W)$, then $\alpha(V \times W) = \{u_0\}$. (If the two coordinate axes collapse to a point, the whole product collapses to that point.)
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:rigidity_theorem}
  After extending scalars to $\bar k = k^{al}$ (the hypotheses persist), assume $k$ algebraically closed; $V$ is then connected. Let $U_0$ be an open affine neighbourhood of $u_0$. By completeness of $V$ the projection $q \colon V \times W \to W$ is closed, so $Z := q(\alpha^{-1}(U \smallsetminus U_0))$ is closed in $W$, and $w \notin Z \iff \alpha(V \times \{w\}) \subseteq U_0$. Since $w_0 \notin Z$, the open set $W \smallsetminus Z$ is non-empty; for $w \in W \smallsetminus Z$ the image $\alpha(V \times \{w\})$ is a complete subset of the affine $U_0$, hence a single point, namely $\alpha(v_0, w) = u_0$. Thus $\alpha$ is constant on the dense open $V \times (W \smallsetminus Z)$ of the irreducible $V \times W$; as $U$ is separated, $\alpha$ agrees with the constant map $u_0$ on all of $V \times W$.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}
[Maps out of a product of complete varieties to an abelian variety]
  \label{cor:complete_product_to_AV_decomp}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Corollary 1.5, \S I.1, p.~9 (PDF p.~15--16)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "COROLLARY 1.5. Let $V$ and $W$ be complete varieties over
  % $k$ with $k$-rational points $v_0$ and $w_0$, and let $p$ and $q$ be the
  % projection maps $V \times W \to V$ and $V \times W \to W$. Let $A$ be an
  % abelian variety. Then a morphism $h\colon V \times W \to A$ such that
  % $h(v_0,w_0) = 0$ can be written uniquely as $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$
  % with $f\colon V \to A$ and $g\colon W \to A$ morphisms such that
  % $f(v_0) = 0$ and $g(w_0) = 0$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Set $f = h|V \times \{w_0\}$, $g = h|\{v_0\} \times
  % W$;" (proof opening, p.~15; remainder on PDF p.~16 not transcribed here ---
  % it applies the Rigidity Theorem 1.1 to the difference $h - f\circ p -
  % g\circ q$, which vanishes on both coordinate axes.)
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary 1.5.}
  \uses{lem:rigidity_theorem}
  Let $V$, $W$ be complete varieties over $k$ with $k$-rational points $v_0$, $w_0$, with projections $p \colon V \times W \to V$, $q \colon V \times W \to W$, and let $A$ be an abelian variety. Every morphism $h \colon V \times W \to A$ with $h(v_0, w_0) = 0$ decomposes uniquely as $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$ with $f \colon V \to A$, $g \colon W \to A$ satisfying $f(v_0) = 0$ and $g(w_0) = 0$. The decomposition follows by applying \cref{lem:rigidity_theorem} to $h - f\circ p - g\circ q$, which kills both coordinate axes.
\end{corollary}

\begin{lemma}
[Rational maps from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extend]
  \label{lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem 3.2, \S I.3, p.~16 (PDF p.~23)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map $\alpha\colon V \dashrightarrow A$
  % from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of
  % $V$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
  % (Theorem 3.1: a rational map from a normal variety to a complete variety is
  % defined off a closed subset of codimension $\geq 2$, p.~16; Lemma 3.3: for a
  % rational map to a group variety the indeterminacy locus, if non-empty, is
  % pure of codimension 1, p.~17.)
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem 3.2.}
  A rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$ from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of $V$ (a genuine morphism). Combine Milne's Theorem~3.1 (rational maps from a normal variety to a complete variety are defined off codimension~$\geq 2$) with Lemma~3.3 (for a rational map to a group variety the indeterminacy locus, if non-empty, is pure of codimension~$1$); the two are compatible only if the indeterminacy locus is empty.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proposition}
[Rational maps from a unirational variety to an abelian variety are constant]
  \label{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10, \S I.3, p.~20 (PDF p.~26)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha\colon V
  % \dashrightarrow A$ from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is
  % constant."
  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically
  % closed field is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map
  % $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can
  % be embedded into $k(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A
  % variety $V$ over an arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if
  % $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By
  % assumption there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense
  % image, and the composite of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism
  % $\beta\colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction
  % argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are regular maps
  % $\beta_i\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1,\ldots,x_d) = \sum
  % \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition 3.10.}
  \uses{lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends, cor:complete_product_to_AV_decomp}
  A variety $V$ is \emph{unirational} if (over $\bar k$) there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$ from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant, cor:complete_product_to_AV_decomp, lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends}
  We may assume $k$ algebraically closed. The dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ composed with $\alpha$ extends (by \cref{lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends}, after compactifying each factor) to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction starting from \cref{cor:complete_product_to_AV_decomp} writes $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ for regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; the one-factor case (a morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$, killed by the Rigidity Theorem applied to its difference of values) shows each $\beta_i$ is constant, hence $\beta$, hence $\alpha$, is constant.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}
[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 specialised to $\mathbb P^1$, \S I.3, p.~20 (PDF p.~26)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha\colon V
  % \dashrightarrow A$ from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is
  % constant."
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition 3.10 (specialised to $\mathbb P^1$).}
  \uses{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant, lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends}
  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field, $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and $A$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $f(p) = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point $p$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. This is the project's committed proof route (route~(c)) for \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant, lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends, prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$, a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve has a $\bar k$-rational point and is therefore isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, which is a rational --- hence unirational --- nonsingular variety. The morphism $f$ is in particular a rational map $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \dashrightarrow A$, so \cref{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant} (Milne, Proposition~3.10) shows $f$ is constant; \cref{lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends} guarantees that there is no indeterminacy to worry about (the rational map is everywhere defined). The pointed hypothesis $f(p) = \eta_A$ pins the constant value to $\eta_A$, so $f$ equals the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. The argument invokes no differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre duality, and no Picard representability, and is valid in arbitrary characteristic --- once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis currently carried by \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} be dropped.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}\leanok
[Genus-$0$ Albanese witness]
  \label{def:genusZeroWitness}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}
  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, thm:rigidity_over_kbar, def:genus}
  Let $C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then there exists a $\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C$ whose underlying scheme is $\Spec k$ (the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$), with the trivial group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension $0$, properness, and geometric irreducibility, and whose $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$ field encodes the Albanese universal property for every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$.
\end{theorem}

This chapter introduces the project's named declaration for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-$0$ Albanese-witness argument: rigidity of morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, \textbf{over an algebraically closed base field $\bar k$ of characteristic zero} (the alg-closed pivot of \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot" below; descent of the constancy conclusion to a general base field $k$ is a separate downstream step, hosted in the genus-$0$ witness chapter). The classical statement (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4) is

\begin{quote}
  \emph{Let $A$ be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$. Then every morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant.}

\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.

The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.

The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing
with the classification of genus-$0$ curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1},
$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA
$\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully decoupled from Route~A.

\medskip

Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.

\section{The Rigidity Lemma}

\begin{lemma}
[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
% assertion."
\begin{proof}
  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
  open subset of $X \times Y$.

  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.

  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
  agree everywhere. \qedhere
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of $X$ makes
  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
  irreducible.)
\end{remark}

\section{The theorem of the cube (deferred deep input)}

\textbf{Deferred prerequisite.} The block below records the theorem of the cube as the heavy,
\emph{not-yet-built} characteristic-free input that the single-curve base case of
\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} ultimately rests on. We give Mumford's statement
verbatim but \emph{no} formal proof: in Mumford the proof spans \S6 (the splitting
$T(X_0 \times \cdots \times X_n) = \operatorname{Im}\alpha \oplus \operatorname{Ker}\beta$
exhibiting $\Pic$ as a quadratic functor, then the seesaw principle), and the
characteristic-free scheme-theoretic form is Chapter~III \S10. This is the one genuinely
deep, un-formalised prerequisite of route~(c); it carries no Lean target yet.

\begin{theorem}
[Theorem of the cube]
  \label{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Theorem (of the cube), Ch.~II \S6 ``The theorem of the cube: I'', book p.~55 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 66)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM. Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties, $Z$ any variety and $x_0$,
  % $y_0$ and $z_0$ base points on $X$, $Y$, and $Z$, respectively. If $L$ is any line bundle
  % on $X \times Y \times Z$ whose restrictions to each of $\{x_0\} \times Y \times Z$,
  % $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$ and $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, $L$ is trivial."
  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S6, Theorem of the cube, p.~55.}
  Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties and $Z$ any variety over $\bar k$, with base points
  $x_0 \in X$, $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$. If $L$ is a line bundle on $X \times Y \times Z$
  whose restrictions to each of $\{x_0\} \times Y \times Z$, $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$, and
  $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, then $L$ is trivial. \emph{(Recorded as a deferred
  input; no formal proof in this project yet --- this is the heavy un-built prerequisite of
  route~(c).)}
\end{theorem}

\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}

\begin{proposition}
[A morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant]
  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single $\mathbb P^1$ factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an
  % arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
  single $\bar k$-point.
\end{proposition}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By assumption
% there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense image, and the composite
% of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
% regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
% \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.

  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the
  complete nonsingular variety $\mathbb P^1$, so there is no indeterminacy to resolve (a
  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne
  Theorem~3.2; here $f$ is already a morphism).

  \textbf{The base case is where the depth lies.} The constancy of $f$ is the statement that
  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no
  rational curves''. Over an arbitrary field this does \emph{not} follow from
  \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} alone: the Rigidity Lemma collapses a \emph{product}
  $X \times Y \to Z$ when one coordinate axis is already collapsed, but for a single map
  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ no coordinate axis is collapsed a priori (the difference map
  $(x, y) \mapsto f(x) - f(y)$ on $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ is constant on an axis only
  if $f$ is already constant). The characteristic-free input that supplies the missing fact
  is the theorem of the cube (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}): it forces the additive
  structure of $\Pic$ and, with it, the triviality of the line bundles a nonconstant
  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ would produce, so that no such nonconstant morphism exists. (In
  characteristic $0$ one may instead pull back an invariant differential and use
  $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0$; that argument fails in characteristic $p$, which is exactly
  why the cube is the committed characteristic-free engine.)

  \textbf{Conclusion.} Granting the base case, $f$ is constant with value $f(\infty) = 0_A$
  (before the normalising translation, the constant value is the original $f(\infty)$). For
  the multi-factor statement of Proposition~3.10 (a general unirational $V$), one writes
  $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition (Milne
  Corollary~1.5, itself a corollary of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) and applies the base case to
  each $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
  \label{rmk:cube_is_load_bearing}
  The Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) is genuinely used in the surrounding
  machinery --- it yields Milne's Corollary~1.5 (additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(- , A)$ over a
  product of complete pointed varieties), which drives the multi-factor induction of
  Proposition~3.10 and which proves that maps of abelian varieties are homomorphisms up to
  translation. But the \emph{single-curve} base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' is
  not a formal consequence of the Rigidity Lemma by itself; in characteristic-free form it
  rests on \cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}. This refines the iter-156 expectation that a single
  $\mathbb P^1$ factor would be cube-free: the multi-factor \emph{induction} is cube-free
  (it is pure Rigidity-Lemma additivity), but the base of that induction is not. See the
  Strategy-modifying findings of this chapter's writer report.
\end{remark}

\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}

\begin{proposition}
[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
  \uses{def:genus}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
\end{proposition}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:genus}
  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}

  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}
  \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} is Riemann--Roch--flavoured and its formalisation is a
  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
\end{remark}

\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-$0$ curve}

\begin{theorem}
[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.

  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
  \eta[A]$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.

  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
  Once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis carried by
  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.
\end{proof}

# Blueprint Writer Report

## Slug
av-rigidity-chapter

## Status

single-$\mathbb P^1$ base case is NOT cube-free, refining the iter-156 expectation).

blueprint/src/chapters/AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex  (NEW FILE — created)

## Determination of the minimal chain (the directive's "most important job")
I read Mumford §4 (PDF p.54, the Rigidity Lemma + proof) and §6 (PDF p.66, the theorem of
the cube), Milne Thm 1.1 (PDF p.14) and Prop 3.10 (PDF p.26, statement + proof), and
Hartshorne Example IV.1.3.5 (PDF p.314). Conclusion on the cube question:

- **The Rigidity Lemma (Mumford Form I) is cube-free** — its proof is purely a
  completeness/closed-map argument (`p₂` closed since `X` complete; a proper connected
  variety has no nonconstant map to an affine). Confirmed verbatim. It is the natural
  formalisation entry point.
- **The single-$\mathbb P^1$ constancy is NOT a consequence of the Rigidity Lemma alone.**
  The Rigidity Lemma collapses a *product* `X × Y → Z` when one axis is already collapsed;
  a bare `f : ℙ¹ → A` presents no collapsed axis (the difference map `(x,y) ↦ f(x)−f(y)` is
  constant on an axis only if `f` already is). Mumford himself leaves "no rational curves /
  `ℙ¹ → A` constant" to the reader (the reference card confirms it is *not* a labelled §4
  result), and Milne's Prop 3.10 proof bottoms out at "the lemma shows each `βᵢ` is
  constant" — i.e. the single-curve base case is taken as known. In characteristic-free
  form that base case rests on the **theorem of the cube** (the char-0 alternative,
  `f*ω` + `H⁰(ℙ¹,Ω)=0`, is exactly the Frobenius-wall route the project abandoned).
- **What IS cube-free is the multi-factor induction**: Milne Cor 1.5 (additivity of
  `Hom(−,A)` over a product of complete pointed varieties) is a corollary of the Rigidity
  Lemma, and drives `β(x₁,…,x_d) = Σ βᵢ(xᵢ)`. So the directive's framing was half right:
  the *induction* is cube-free, the *base* is not.

I therefore structured the chapter around the minimal chain with the cube retained as a
clearly-flagged **deferred deep input** (matching the project memory chain "thm of cube →
Rigidity 1.1 → … → Prop 3.10"), and recorded the refutation in
`\cref{rmk:cube_is_load_bearing}` and in Strategy-modifying findings below.

## Strategy-modifying findings
**The single-$\mathbb P^1$ base case of the genus-0 rigidity arm is NOT cube-free.** The
iter-156 disposition (recorded in project memory and echoed in the directive) expected that a
single `ℙ¹ → A` factor would avoid the theorem of the cube, with the cube entering only the
multi-factor unirational induction. Reading the sources shows the opposite split:

- The **multi-factor induction** (Milne Prop 3.10's `β = Σ βᵢ`) *is* cube-free — it is pure
  Rigidity-Lemma additivity (Milne Cor 1.5).

char-free scheme form §10), not the elementary Rigidity Lemma. The Rigidity Lemma alone does
not close the genus-0 arm. STRATEGY.md should reflect that the genus-0 arm's keystone
prerequisite is the cube (a substantial multi-section build), and that `thm:rigidity_lemma`
(prover-ready at iter-158) is necessary but not sufficient — closing
